clearing_status.rationale: The SEC formally extended its original compliance deadlines by one year in February 2025—to Dec. 31, 2026 (cash) and June 30, 2027 (repo)—constituting a confirmed partial delay. As of April–May 2026, open exemptive relief requests (from SIFMA on inter-affiliate transactions and from IIB on cross-border trades) remain pending, signaling continued industry pushback and further potential carve-outs, but no second broad extension has been announced and the SEC stated in September 2025 it does not intend further extensions.

jgb30.rationale: TradingEconomics reported the Japan 30-year JGB yield at 3.88% on June 24, 2026, up 0.03 percentage points from the prior session; no same-day (June 29) print was retrievable from search results, though the broader JGB market has been drifting slightly lower in the days since.

plan_financing.rationale: As of late June 2026, Japan's 10-year JGB yield stands around 2.61–2.65%, reflecting persistent yield pressure tied to Takaichi's large fiscal stimulus program and BOJ rate-hike expectations, though yields have eased modestly in recent sessions. The May 2026 supplementary budget pledged no net new borrowing to reassure markets, but ongoing concerns over consumption-tax cuts and rising debt-servicing costs leave the financing path unclear rather than disorderly.

direct_lending_default.rationale: KBRA (Jan 2026) projects a 2% direct-lending default rate by volume for 2026 (vs. 1.5% in 2025), consistent with Moody's April 2026 lower-bound estimate of ~1.6–4.7% for 2025 depending on whether distressed exchanges are included. The headline/conventional payment-default rate converges near 2%, though adjusted rates including liability management exercises approach 3–5%.

redemption_gates.rationale: Multiple major private credit funds — including Blackstone BCRED ($79B), BlackRock HPS ($26B), Blue Owl OCIC ($36B), Apollo Debt Solutions ($26B), Ares, Cliffwater, and Morgan Stanley — have all imposed 5% quarterly redemption caps in Q1–Q2 2026 amid redemption requests ranging from 10% to over 40%, with Bloomberg describing this as 'widespread' gating across the largest funds in the industry and the CRS noting it has drawn congressional attention.

new_imf_programs.rationale: IMF program activity in 2026 shows ongoing and augmented ECF arrangements (Burkina Faso augmentation requested May 2026, Ghana shifting to PCI, DRC reviews completed), with the Middle East conflict cited as a new balance-of-payments shock straining frontier/EM sovereigns; however, no confirmed cluster of fresh sovereign defaults or missed coupons was found in 2026, placing the indicator at rising distress (1) rather than calm or crisis.

china_ppi.rationale: Trading Economics (sourcing NBS, released 2026-06-10) reports China PPI rose +3.9% YoY in May 2026, the third consecutive monthly gain and fastest pace since July 2022. This positive print is re-verified: NBS confirmed the PPI exited a 41-month deflation streak in March 2026 (+0.5%), and continued accelerating in April (+2.8%) and May (+3.9%), driven by surging global commodity and energy prices.

trade_actions.rationale: The US-China trade environment is actively rising but not in full re-escalation: the USTR proposed a new 12.5% Section 301 tariff on China (June 2, 2026) tied to a forced-labor investigation, new anti-dumping actions on Chinese goods (van-type trailers, solar cells, MSG) were issued through mid-June 2026, and China on June 22 placed 10 U.S. companies on its export control list and banned 46 defense contractors from procurement—yet both sides remain within a managed truce framework, with China stopping short of broad counter-tariffs.

license_flow.rationale: China's rare earth magnet export licensing regime is selective and actively managed rather than frozen or fully open: a non-automatic licensing system with whitelisted exporters and extended review periods is in force, general licences have been issued only to a handful of major producers (e.g., JL Mag), the strictest October 2025 extraterritorial rules remain suspended until November 2026, and the June 22, 2026 addition of MP Materials and USA Rare Earth to the MOFCOM export control entity list represents continued targeted tightening—but not a blanket freeze on magnet exports overall.

truce_status.rationale: The November 2025 truce (suspending China's Oct 2025 rare earth export controls until Nov 10, 2026) remains nominally in place, but on June 22, 2026 China added MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — the two largest U.S. rare earth producers — to its export control blacklist, with analysts and FDD stating 'the short pause in U.S.-China minerals warfare is over' and SCMP quoting experts that 'Beijing's new critical mineral export restrictions underscore how fragile the bilateral truce is.'

hree_premium.rationale: Benchmark Minerals (March 2026) documented dysprosium CIF North America at 4.4x EXW China in 2025, with the multiple widening into 2026; corroborating data from Strategic Metals Invest (June 2026) shows terbium at ~$4,028/kg ex-China vs ~$804/kg domestic China (~5x) and dysprosium at ~$930/kg vs ~$191/kg (~4.9x), supporting a mid-range estimate of ~4.5x. No single authoritative FastMarkets or Adamas Intelligence figure was directly accessible.

peg_deviation.rationale: Live price feeds (Bitget, Yahoo Finance) show USDT trading at approximately $0.9983–$0.9988 on June 29, 2026, a deviation of ~0.17% below peg; USDC is trading at $1.00 with no meaningful deviation. No major stress event for USDT or USDC is evident; the only recent notable depeg (apxUSD to $0.93 on June 4 per CoinDesk) involved a minor niche stablecoin, not a top-tier one.

stablecoin_supply.rationale: StablecoinBeat reports $307.5B total stablecoin market cap by June 2026, while CoinDesk and MacroMicro reported ~$321-322B as of May 24-26, 2026; the June figure reflects a modest pullback, yielding an estimate of ~$310B. Multiple sources corroborate a range of $307–$322B for late May/June 2026.

genius_impl.rationale: The GENIUS Act was signed into law July 18, 2025, but as of late June 2026 all major implementing rules (OCC, FDIC, FinCEN/OFAC, Treasury) remain in proposed/comment stages rather than finalized, with final rules targeted for July 2026 and the full regime not operational until January 2027—creating a compliance gap period with no final licensing or AML framework yet in force. Divergences between agency proposals (e.g., FDIC vs. OCC on reserve-failure consequences) and unresolved issues (yield/rewards standoff, secondary-market SAR obligations, state 'substantial similarity' standards) constitute material gaps and compliance friction consistent with a score of 1.

ai_revenue_gap.rationale: Hyperscaler 2026 capex has surged ~77% YoY to ~$725B while AI revenues are accelerating (Google Cloud +63% YoY to $20B/quarter, Azure AI at >$37B run rate, cloud contract backlogs doubling) — analysts at Jefferies called the monetization thesis 'garbage'-proof. The gap remains wide (e.g., Microsoft's ~$190B capex vs. ~$25B targeted AI revenue), and FCF is compressing, but revenue growth is broadly keeping pace with expectations, making this a wide-but-stable gap rather than a widening-or-disappointment scenario.

gpu_refi_stress.rationale: Multiple sources through mid-2026 document meaningful early-stage strain: banks are offloading AI data-center construction debt at discounts as exposure nears internal limits, CoreWeave carries a $4.2B refinancing wall in 2026 against depreciated GPU collateral, and analysts have flagged the 'GPU debt treadmill' and collateral bifurcation as structural risks — but no acute default or distressed restructuring of GPU-backed debt has been publicly confirmed yet.

cmbs_office_delinq.rationale: Trepp data reported by ConnectCRE and Wolf Street confirmed the CMBS office delinquency rate hit an all-time high of 12.34% in January 2026, up 103 bps month-over-month; no more recent office-specific breakdown (Feb–May 2026) was retrievable from public sources.

regional_bank_stress.rationale: The FDIC confirms only 2 bank failures in 2026 (Metropolitan Capital Bank & Trust in January and Community Bank & Trust – West Georgia in May), both small firm-specific cases unrelated to a systemic CRE cluster; however, regional banks continue to face elevated CRE stress—particularly in office lending—with S&P Global projecting loan-loss provisions rising to 24% of net revenue in 2026 and analysts characterizing the pattern as 'individual institution events rather than systemic episodes.'

govt_stability.rationale: Lecornu's minority government has survived multiple no-confidence votes and passed the 2026 budget via Article 49.3 in February 2026, but it remains fragile in a hung parliament with persistent budget deficit concerns and ongoing threats from both hard-left and far-right blocs. No snap election has been called as of late June 2026, but the threat remains non-negligible.

rating_action.rationale: Moody's changed France's outlook to negative from stable in October 2025 (affirming Aa3), while both Fitch (September 2025) and S&P lowered France's rating to A+ with stable outlooks post-downgrade. The active Moody's negative outlook is the most recent agency-level signal still in force.

subprime_auto_dpd.rationale: Per Fitch Ratings data cited by Wolf Street (May 19, 2026), the subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency rate peaked at a record 6.90% in January 2026 and edged down to 6.80% in February 2026; March data had not yet been released as of that report.

bnpl_delinq.rationale: Survey data (LendingTree, March 2026) shows 47% of BNPL users reported a late payment in the past year, up from 41% in 2025 and 34% prior, and the Richmond Fed's February 2026 brief confirms 'indicative evidence from consumer surveys that late payment and delinquency behavior among BNPL users has increased since 2023.' However, Affirm's 30+ day delinquency rate held flat at 2.8% YoY as of Q1 2026, formal charge-off rates remain low (~1.8–2%), and the Richmond Fed found no clear evidence of acute systemic stress — ruling out a score of 2.

dollar_reserve_share.rationale: IMF COFER data published March 27, 2026 reports the USD share of global FX reserves at 56.77% in Q4 2025, down from 56.93% in Q3 2025. This is the most recent official IMF COFER release.

weaponization_event.rationale: In 2026, BRICS nations have advanced new non-dollar settlement frameworks—including the operational deployment of BRICS Pay and the launch of a gold-backed 'Unit' settlement token—driven explicitly by reserve weaponization concerns (e.g., the $300B Russian reserve freeze); simultaneously, the US is actively discussing using frozen Iranian assets for Gulf reconstruction. However, no single landmark new sovereign reserve freeze or a completed, system-wide settlement shift has occurred; the moves remain in the 'new agreements/active expansion of alternatives' tier rather than a decisive rupture.

insurer_exits.rationale: Non-renewals remain elevated across at least 13 states (CA, FL, TX, NC, OK, NE, MN, GA and others) driven by climate risk and ZIP-code-level AI underwriting, with the trend described as ongoing but not sharply accelerating — in fact, some markets (FL, reinsurance broadly) are stabilizing, AM Best revised the US homeowners segment outlook from negative to stable, and survey data shows carrier-initiated non-renewals ticked up only modestly in 2026.

cat_losses.rationale: Q1 2026 global insured catastrophe losses totaled $20 billion, 26% below the 10-year average with no single event exceeding $10 billion (Gallagher Re). The 2026 Atlantic hurricane season is forecast as near to below-average (El Niño conditions), and no major wildfire or hurricane loss event has been flagged by Aon, Swiss Re, or Munich Re for 2026 YTD.

ss_reform_news.rationale: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026) projects OASI trust fund depletion in 2032 — one year sooner than prior estimates — warning of an automatic 22% benefit cut absent congressional action; CRFB, SSA, Brookings, and Bipartisan Policy Center are all actively sounding alarms and debating reform options, but no concrete benefit cuts or emergency legislation has been enacted.

pension_leverage_event.rationale: As of mid-2026, authoritative sources (TPR, ESMA June 2026, Russell Investments) show LDI leverage has been materially reduced—LDI market size fell from ~£1.5tn to ~£0.7tn, buffers now exceed 300bps, and 'LDI related operations have been normal' through 2025 interest rate volatility episodes. ESMA's June 2026 report notes a residual subset of funds could face liquidity challenges under a large shock, but there is no evidence of active margin stress or forced-selling in 2026.

funded_ratio.rationale: Milliman's Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI), released June 3, 2026, reported the funded ratio for the 100 largest US public defined benefit plans at 87.6% as of April 30, 2026, rebounding from 83.7% in March on strong April investment returns of 4.7%. This is corroborated by Equable Institute's December 31, 2025 figure of 82.5% for US state and local plans, published June 26, 2026.

interconnection_months.rationale: LBNL's 'Queued Up: 2025 Edition' (data through end of 2024) reports the typical project reaching commercial operation in 2024 spent 55 months (4.5 years) in the queue; the official LBNL/emp.lbl.gov page corroborates that median IR-to-COD duration has exceeded 4 years for projects built in 2018–2024. Confidence is medium rather than high because the 55-month figure comes from a third-party summary of the LBNL report rather than the primary PDF, and the official LBNL page states 'over 4 years' without a precise month count.

dc_moratoria.rationale: At least 12 states introduced moratorium bills in 2026; New York's legislature passed a first-in-nation one-year statewide moratorium on hyperscale data centers (June 4, 2026, awaiting governor's signature), dozens of municipalities enacted local construction pauses (Minneapolis, Denver, Baltimore, Seattle, Reno, etc.), and signed laws in Florida, Oklahoma, and Washington impose strict cost-allocation and clean-energy requirements—constituting 'widespread bans or major project cancellations' per the rubric.

ai_power_pressure.rationale: The IEA (April 2026) confirms data centre electricity demand grew 17% in 2025 with grid bottlenecks 'tightening,' slow connection queues forcing developers toward onsite gas generation, and ~20% of planned projects at risk of delays; Wood Mackenzie (cited in Fortune, March 2026) notes utilities lack capacity to build fast enough, and nearly 50% of 2026 projects face power-related delays — consistent with 'tight' (1) but without confirmed widespread curtailments or declared emergencies that would warrant level 2.

mine_disruptions.rationale: Grasberg (world's second-largest copper mine) has been operating at ~35% reduced capacity since a September 2025 mudslide with full restart pushed to 2028, while Kamoa-Kakula also suffered seismic disruptions in 2025 reducing its 2026 guidance; together with El Teniente and Chilean output falling ~9% YoY in March 2026, multiple major institutions (Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan, ING, BofA) forecast a 2026 refined copper deficit of 330,000–600,000 tonnes, a clear cluster of major outages tightening supply.

copper_deficit_timing.rationale: The ICSG's April 2026 Copper Market Forecast 2026/2027 reversed its October 2025 deficit projection, now forecasting a ~96,000 mt surplus for 2026 due to weaker demand growth (revised down to 1.6%) and higher secondary refined copper output; this is ICSG's official current stance as confirmed by icsg.org and multiple corroborating sources.

drought_chip_farm.rationale: The US Southwest faces an unprecedented snow drought (snowpack 32–53% below previous record lows as of April 1, 2026) driving critical water shortages along the Colorado River, with heat and dry weather worsening conditions through late June 2026. In Taiwan, TSMC's CEO publicly admitted in June 2026 that just the prior month he was considering emergency water trucks, flagging water as an emerging constraint for chip fabs — though recent rains provided short-term relief and no production cutbacks have been announced.

reservoir_stress.rationale: As of June 26, 2026, Lake Mead sits at ~1,045 feet — only 10 feet above the critical 1,035-foot threshold where Hoover Dam loses 70% of its power capacity, at just 29–35% of total capacity and 47% of the date average, with USBR projecting a continued decline toward record-low 1,015 feet by July 2027 amid severe upstream drought conditions (inflow at 15% of average).

water_dispute.rationale: The Indus Waters Treaty has been unilaterally suspended by India since April 2025, with India's water minister stating in June 2026 that it would work to ensure Pakistan receives 'not a single drop' of Indus water; Pakistan's Defence Minister simultaneously threatened war over the dispute. On the GERD/Nile front, Ethiopia has completed its dam inauguration and continues unilateral operation while Egypt and Sudan reject this as a violation of international law, though no armed conflict has occurred.

fao_food_trend.rationale: The FAO Food Price Index averaged 130.8 points in May 2026, essentially flat from April's revised 131.0—itself a post-January-2023 high reached after three consecutive monthly increases; the index is materially elevated and on a rising trend, though the latest month showed marginal stabilisation rather than a sharp spike.

china_export_curbs.rationale: China's NDRC suspended phosphate fertilizer exports through August 2026 and extended restrictions to nitrogen-potassium blends in March 2026; while a tightly capped urea quota (~1.5–1.6Mt, June–August, with strict $660/t price floors) was issued in late May 2026, broad curbs on urea and phosphate remain firmly in place per the Food Security Portal and multiple trade sources.

hormuz_fertilizer.rationale: As of June 29, 2026, the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to normal commercial shipping (only ~5 vessels/day vs. ~93 pre-war), with fertilizer, ammonia, and sulfur flows severely disrupted since late February; a US-Iran MOU signed June 17 has yielded only partial reopening under naval escort, with war-risk insurance at 8× pre-crisis, mines uncleared, and Iran's June 20 re-closure declaration creating renewed uncertainty. One-third of global fertilizer trade remains stalled, urea prices are up ~50%, and the UN established a dedicated task force to facilitate humanitarian fertilizer shipments through the strait.

taiwan_tension.rationale: The Taiwan Strait remains in a state of elevated — not crisis — tension: large-scale PLA drills (Justice Mission 2025, Dec 2025) rehearsed a full maritime blockade and disrupted civilian air/sea traffic, and China's Coast Guard and PLAN have been conducting assertive patrols east of Taiwan as recently as June 2026, while Taiwan conducted its first-ever HIMARS live-fire into the Strait on June 10, 2026; however, no blockade, kinetic clash, or full mobilization has been confirmed.

export_controls.rationale: Multiple major escalations are active simultaneously: the bipartisan MATCH Act (introduced April 2026) proposes banning all DUV lithography shipments to China, U.S. authorities raised export-control violation allegations against ASML (triggering an ~8% stock drop in late June 2026), and China enacted sweeping tech-transfer/retaliation regulations on June 11, 2026, granting explicit legal authority to counter nations that restrict Chinese technology—with ASML, Lam Research, and Applied Materials equipment in Chinese fabs identified as obvious targets. China also added 10 U.S. firms to its export-control list on June 22, 2026, and issued a new strategic-minerals whistleblower enforcement mechanism effective July 1, 2026.

fab_concentration.rationale: Meaningful diversification is actively progressing — TSMC's Arizona Fab 1 is profitable, Japan's JASM posted its first quarterly profit in Q1 2026, a second Arizona fab targets 3nm mass production in H2 2027, and Germany's fab is under construction — but Taiwan still dominates leading-edge capacity today, with the Southern Taiwan Science Park handling the majority of current 3nm production and overseas fabs still in early ramp stages, leaving concentration still high in the near term.

hormuz_redsea_status.rationale: A June 17 US-Iran MOU aimed at reopening the Strait was signed, but tit-for-tat US and Iranian strikes over the weekend of June 27-28 renewed hostilities; Washington and Tehran agreed on June 28-29 to halt attacks and resume talks in Doha. Commercial transit remains far below normal (~5% of pre-crisis volume per IMF PortWatch as of June 21), convoys are moving under naval escort, and Brent crude at ~$73/barrel is only modestly elevated (~127 cents above pre-war levels), consistent with a contested/de-escalating scenario rather than a full closure. Under the rubric, the active MOU/ceasefire framework and naval-escorted convoys cap this at 1.

cable_incidents.rationale: Submarine cable cuts in Saudi waters near Jeddah (SMW4 and IMEWE) confirmed by PTCL caused internet service disruptions in Pakistan as of ~June 2026; separately, Red Sea and Hormuz cables remain unrepaired from late-2025 cuts with repair vessels unable to operate in the war zone, causing ongoing connectivity degradation across the Middle East and South Asia.

treasury_clearing_rollout.rationale: As of late June 2026, implementation is proceeding toward the Dec 31, 2026 (cash) and Jun 30, 2027 (repo) deadlines—already extended one year due to prior unreadiness—but material friction persists: the SEC is still processing open exemptive-relief requests on inter-affiliate and extraterritorial scoping (comment deadline May 29, 2026), the TBAC May 2026 report cites legal documentation volume as the 'largest challenge,' Europe/Asia participants are largely beyond scoping, only 54% of firms express confidence in meeting the cash deadline, and 29% of buy-side firms do not expect to complete preparations in time.
